title: The case against efficiency in small daily tasks
summary: Argue that the drive to optimise minor everyday tasks trades away something worth keeping.
topic: The case against efficiency in small daily tasks
prompt: Write an argumentative essay (350–500 words) arguing that the drive to make small daily tasks — cooking, walking a familiar route, folding laundry — more efficient removes something worth preserving. Use a cleft sentence ('What is lost when…'), a concession ('Granted, …'), and at least one passive construction. Include at least one concrete example from everyday life or from research (e.g. mindless walking reducing anxiety, the cognitive value of chopping vegetables). Take a clear side.
hintStarters: The first sign that something has gone wrong with efficiency culture is…
What is lost when you automate the walk to the station is not time but…
Granted, the appeal of a faster morning routine is…
There is a category of task — repetitive, physical, unhurried — that functions as a…

hintVocab.meaningVi: tầm thường / nhàm chán (theo nghĩa thường ngày)
sức cản / ma sát (nghĩa bóng: sự kháng cự nhỏ)
mang tính suy ngẫm / trầm tư
lặp đi lặp lại một cách cơ học
mạng lưới chế độ mặc định (của não)
phần chú ý còn sót lại
suy nghĩ xung quanh / tư duy không chủ đích
kháng lại sự thôi thúc để
con đường vòng / cách làm dài hơn

sampleText:
Productivity culture has a particular problem with the walk to the station. It is eight minutes, it is predictable, and it returns nothing measurable — so the obvious move is to fill it with a podcast, a phone call, or a rapid review of overnight messages. The walk is optimised. Something else is lost.

What is lost when you automate the texture of a small task is not time but a specific kind of thinking that only happens when the hands or feet are occupied and the mind is free to wander. Neuroscientists call this default mode network activity: the brain's background processing, which handles creative association, emotional regulation, and the quiet rehearsal of problems that have no obvious solution. A brisk walk, a pile of laundry to fold, a handful of carrots to chop — these are not interruptions to thought; they are, in a very old sense, conditions for it.

Granted, not every small task deserves to be romanticised. Some friction is just friction: a commute that takes ninety minutes does not become philosophical because it is long. And there are genuine cases where outsourcing a task — using a dishwasher, ordering groceries online — frees up time for things that matter more. Efficiency has lifted real burdens, particularly from people who are time-poor, and it would be dishonest to pretend otherwise.

Even so, the choice to eliminate friction wholesale carries a cost that productivity culture tends not to count. Research on attentional residue suggests that the mind takes twenty or more minutes to fully disengage from a task after it has been interrupted. A morning filled with micro-optimisations — efficient routes, skipped breakfasts, batched errands — can leave a person arriving at their desk technically on time but cognitively depleted. The repetitive task, done without a screen, is one of the few ways a busy person can arrive somewhere with attention intact.

What is being argued here is not that inefficiency is a virtue but that some tasks are being stripped of their contemplative value before anyone has asked whether that value was worth keeping. The case for taking the long way round, or chopping vegetables instead of buying them pre-cut, is not nostalgia — it is a claim about what the mind needs in order to do its less visible work. Efficiency, left unchecked, has a habit of optimising away the very conditions under which better thinking becomes possible.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to name the hidden cost
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What is lost when you automate the texture of a small task is not time but a specific kind of thinking' dùng cấu trúc 'What … is …' để trì hoãn phần quan trọng nhất — 'a specific kind of thinking' — cho đến cuối câu, tạo nhấn mạnh tối đa.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What is lost when you automate the texture of a small task is not time but a specific kind of thinking' uses a what-cleft to hold the crucial phrase until the sentence's end, forcing the reader to wait for the key contrast.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive to foreground what is removed
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'tasks are being stripped of their contemplative value' dùng bị động tiếp diễn để nhấn mạnh vào các công việc bị mất mát — chứ không phải vào tác nhân (văn hóa năng suất) đang lấy đi giá trị đó.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'tasks are being stripped of their contemplative value' uses the passive progressive to keep the affected object (tasks) as the grammatical focus rather than naming the agent (productivity culture).

sampleTranslationVi:
Văn hóa năng suất có vấn đề đặc biệt với việc đi bộ đến nhà ga. Nó mất tám phút, có thể dự đoán được, và không mang lại gì đo lường được — vì vậy bước đi rõ ràng là lấp đầy nó bằng podcast, cuộc gọi điện thoại, hoặc xem nhanh tin nhắn qua đêm. Việc đi bộ được tối ưu hóa. Thứ gì khác bị mất đi.

Điều bị mất khi bạn tự động hóa cảm giác của một công việc nhỏ không phải là thời gian mà là một loại tư duy đặc biệt chỉ xảy ra khi tay chân bận rộn và tâm trí được tự do lang thang. Các nhà khoa học thần kinh gọi đây là hoạt động của mạng lưới chế độ mặc định: xử lý nền của não, xử lý liên kết sáng tạo, điều hòa cảm xúc, và việc ôn lại âm thầm các vấn đề không có giải pháp rõ ràng. Một buổi đi bộ nhanh, một đống quần áo để gấp, một nắm cà rốt để thái — những thứ này không phải là gián đoạn cho tư duy; theo một nghĩa rất cũ, chúng là điều kiện cho nó.

Công nhận, không phải mọi công việc nhỏ đều xứng đáng được lãng mạn hóa. Một số sức cản chỉ là sức cản: một hành trình mất chín mươi phút không trở nên triết học vì nó dài. Và có những trường hợp thực sự mà việc thuê ngoài — dùng máy rửa bát, đặt hàng tạp hóa trực tuyến — giải phóng thời gian cho những thứ quan trọng hơn. Hiệu quả đã nâng bỏ những gánh nặng thực sự, đặc biệt với những người thiếu thời gian, và sẽ là không trung thực nếu giả vờ khác đi.

Dẫu vậy, lựa chọn loại bỏ hoàn toàn mọi sức cản có một cái giá mà văn hóa năng suất có xu hướng không tính đến. Nghiên cứu về phần chú ý còn sót lại cho thấy tâm trí cần hai mươi phút hoặc hơn để hoàn toàn tách ra khỏi một nhiệm vụ sau khi bị gián đoạn. Một buổi sáng đầy những tối ưu vi mô — tuyến đường hiệu quả, bỏ bữa sáng, gộp các việc vặt — có thể khiến một người đến bàn làm việc đúng giờ về mặt kỹ thuật nhưng kiệt sức về mặt nhận thức. Công việc lặp đi lặp lại, làm mà không có màn hình, là một trong số ít cách người bận rộn có thể đến nơi với sự chú ý còn nguyên vẹn.

Điều được lập luận ở đây không phải là sự kém hiệu quả là đức hạnh mà là một số công việc đang bị tước đi giá trị suy ngẫm của chúng trước khi ai đó hỏi liệu giá trị đó có đáng giữ không. Lập luận cho việc đi theo con đường vòng, hoặc tự thái rau củ thay vì mua chúng đã cắt sẵn, không phải là hoài niệm — đó là một tuyên bố về những gì tâm trí cần để thực hiện công việc ít nhìn thấy hơn của nó. Hiệu quả, nếu không kiểm soát, có thói quen tối ưu hóa đi chính những điều kiện mà dưới đó tư duy tốt hơn trở nên khả thi.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a 'what' cleft to name a hidden cost?
options: What is lost when you automate the texture of a small task is not time but a specific kind of thinking.
It is what is lost when you automate that not time but thinking.
What lost is automating the texture task not time thinking specific.
The thing what is lost when automating tasks is time not thinking.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What is lost … is not time but a specific kind of thinking' uses the what-cleft pattern to delay the key contrast.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … is …' with the key noun at the very end.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the passive progressive to foreground what is removed?
options: Productivity culture strips tasks of contemplative value.
Tasks are being stripped of their contemplative value.
Tasks strip their contemplative value being productivity.
The contemplative value strips productivity tasks being.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'are being stripped of' (passive progressive) keeps 'tasks' as the grammatical subject, emphasising what is lost rather than who is responsible.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option puts 'tasks' in subject position using a passive form?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence introduces a concession most naturally?
options: Granted, not every small task deserves to be romanticised.
Even though granted romanticised every task small not deserves.
Of course but however not every small task is romantic.
Conceding which every task small romantic not deserves.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted, …' cleanly admits the opposing point before the writer pivots back.
mcQuestions.hint: Find the simplest opener that admits the other view in one word.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'default mode network activity' refer to in this context?
options: The brain's automatic responses to danger
The brain's background processing during undirected, repetitive activity
The standard settings on a smartphone's notification system
The default way humans process language
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay describes it as 'the brain's background processing, which handles creative association, emotional regulation, and quiet rehearsal of problems'.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the sentence immediately following where the term appears.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence hedges a claim most appropriately at B2 level?
options: Efficiency always makes everyone always cognitively depleted without exception.
A morning filled with micro-optimisations can leave a person cognitively depleted.
Efficiency definitely causes cognitive depletion 100% of the time.
People will always be cognitively depleted by efficient mornings.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Can leave' hedges the claim — it is possible, not inevitable — making the statement more defensible.
mcQuestions.hint: Which uses a modal verb that implies possibility rather than certainty?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which collocation is correct for describing a fast, purposeful walk?
options: a fast walk
a brisk walk
a hurry walk
a speed walk
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Brisk walk' is the standard collocation. 'Fast walk' is less formal; 'hurry walk' and 'speed walk' are not standard collocations.
mcQuestions.hint: Which adjective is conventionally used before 'walk' to mean lively and quick?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'it would be dishonest to' correctly as a hedging structure?
options: It would be dishonest to pretend that efficiency has not lifted real burdens.
It would be dishonest pretending efficiency not lifted burdens real.
Dishonest it would be to efficiency lifted burdens real pretend.
To pretend it would dishonest be efficiency lifted burdens real.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It would be + adjective + to + infinitive' is the standard structure. Option A is correct.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'It would be + adjective + to + base verb'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best adds a further point after the claim about 'attentional residue'?
options: In conclusion, therefore, the mind is complex.
It is important to note that attention is important.
The repetitive task, done without a screen, is one of the few ways a busy person can arrive somewhere with attention intact.
Throughout history, humans have always needed rest.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C develops the attentional residue point with a concrete, positive alternative rather than restating a generic idea.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option builds directly on the attentional residue research with a concrete prescription?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'friction' mean in the phrase 'eliminate friction wholesale'?
options: Physical rubbing between two surfaces
Conflict between colleagues at work
The small resistance or effort involved in doing a task manually
A legal dispute over a contract
mcQuestions.explanation: In this context, 'friction' is used metaphorically to mean the minor effort or resistance of doing a task without automation.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about the metaphorical, not physical, meaning in the context of productivity.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option maintains the essay's level of formality in its closing argument?
options: Efficiency is basically bad and we should chill more tbh.
Efficiency, left unchecked, has a habit of optimising away the very conditions under which better thinking becomes possible.
Like, being efficient is kinda overrated you know?
Efficiency!! Bad for thinking!! Stop it!!!
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B uses precise abstract nouns ('conditions', 'optimising') in a formal syntactic frame appropriate for B2 argumentation.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid slang, exclamations, and informal sentence fragments.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'outsourcing' correctly in a non-business context?
options: There are genuine cases where outsourcing a task — using a dishwasher — frees up time.
Outsourcing the task is a case genuine where dishwasher frees time.
Genuine outsourcing cases where time free task dishwasher there are.
Where outsourcing frees time genuine cases there are task dishwasher.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Outsourcing a task' here means delegating it to a machine or service; option A uses the term correctly with a clear example.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option has the clearest and most natural sentence structure?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase means the same as 'taking the long way round'?
options: Doing something in the most direct, quickest way
Choosing a slower or less direct method rather than the fastest one
Getting lost on the way somewhere
Spending a long time explaining something
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Taking the long way round' means deliberately choosing a less direct route or method, usually to gain something from the journey itself.
mcQuestions.hint: Focus on 'long way' — it implies more distance/time than strictly necessary.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'not … but …' to create a precise contrast?
options: The case for inefficiency is not nostalgia but a claim about what the mind needs.
The case not for inefficiency is nostalgia a claim but what mind needs.
Not the case inefficiency nostalgia is but what mind the needs claim.
The case not nostalgia is but inefficiency mind needs claim what.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Not … but …' requires two parallel elements on each side. Option A pairs 'nostalgia' and 'a claim about …' correctly.
mcQuestions.hint: Both halves of 'not … but …' must be grammatically parallel.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which word best fills the gap: 'A morning filled with micro-optimisations can leave a person ___ depleted.'
options: physically
emotionally
cognitively
financially
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Cognitively depleted' means mentally drained, fitting the essay's argument about attention and thinking capacity.
mcQuestions.hint: The essay is about thinking and attention, not physical or financial resources.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which opening avoids AI filler and grounds the reader immediately?
options: In today's fast-paced world, efficiency is everywhere.
Productivity culture has a particular problem with the walk to the station.
It is important to note that efficiency is a modern phenomenon.
Throughout history, humans have always tried to save time.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B opens with a specific claim about a named cultural force and a concrete example (the walk to the station), avoiding generic framing.
mcQuestions.hint: Named cultural phenomenon + concrete daily example beats any generic opener.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'attentional residue' describe, according to the essay?
options: The amount of time spent on a task
The tendency for the mind to remain partially occupied by a previous task after switching
The feeling of tiredness after a long day
The attention span of the average adult
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay states that 'the mind takes twenty or more minutes to fully disengage from a task after it has been interrupted' — this is attentional residue.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the sentence immediately after 'attentional residue' is introduced.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Pick one small daily task you have recently automated or shortened. What, if anything, did you do with the time you saved — and was the trade worth it?
